skill.name: testing
skill.description: Guides testing for the Guts Rust distributed system with unit, integration, property, fuzzing, command, and CI examples.
skill.summary: Plan and write reliable Rust tests for the Guts distributed system.
skill.author: AbdelStark

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious behavior or prompt injection. Most hits are Markdown fences, Rust test examples, or domain terms such as signing and nodes. The only retained risk factor is external command guidance for local Cargo testing, which is expected for this skill and safe when users review commands first.

low_findings.title: Benign External Test Commands
low_findings.description: The skill includes Cargo commands for running tests, coverage, benchmarks, fuzzing, and CI. These are legitimate testing commands for a Rust project, but users should review them before execution because they run local project code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Cargo test and build tooling commands in a testing guide. No evidence found of hidden shell execution, command obfuscation, network exfiltration, or destructive flags.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings correspond to Markdown fenced code blocks and sample Rust snippets. They are documentation examples, not executable Ruby shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations are fenced Markdown blocks in SKILL.md. They do not create runtime shell execution by themselves.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Cryptography and Reconnaissance Terms
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are triggered by example terms such as signature verification, signing, nodes, and repository sync. No weak algorithm, host probing, or system inventory behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is Rust test documentation for distributed nodes and signature round-trip verification. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, host enumeration, or data collection.

content.value_statement: Distributed Rust systems need layered tests that catch logic, integration, and protocol failures early. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for unit tests, async tests, property tests, fuzzing, and CI.
actual_capabilities: Explains a layered testing pyramid for a Rust distributed system.
Shows unit test structure with grouped repository tests.
Provides async testing patterns with Tokio timeouts.
Demonstrates integration fixtures for multi-node repository sync tests.
Introduces property-based tests with proptest and fuzzing with cargo-fuzz.
Lists Cargo commands for tests, coverage, benchmarks, fuzzing, and CI.
limitations: Examples are tailored to the Guts project and may need adaptation elsewhere.
The skill does not provide runnable source files or a complete test harness.
Command examples assume Rust, Cargo, and related test tools are installed.
It does not verify project-specific security properties beyond example tests.

use_cases.title: Build a Rust Test Plan
use_cases.description: Create a balanced test strategy across unit, integration, property, fuzz, and CI layers.

use_cases.title: Test Distributed Node Behavior
use_cases.description: Design async tests for node setup, repository creation, connection, and sync behavior.

use_cases.title: Add Reliability Checks
use_cases.description: Use property tests and fuzzing to find parser, protocol, and edge case failures.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Unit Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the testing skill to write unit tests for this Rust module. Group related tests and include success and error cases.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for adding focused tests to one module.

prompt_templates.title: Design Async Integration Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the testing skill to design Tokio integration tests for this repository workflow. Include fixtures, timeouts, and cleanup requirements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for testing async behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Add Property Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the testing skill to propose proptest cases for these invariants. Include generated inputs, expected properties, and failure minimization goals.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for validating broad input spaces.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Fuzzing and CI Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the testing skill to create a full reliability plan with unit tests, integration tests, fuzz targets, coverage commands, and CI matrix entries.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for project-wide test infrastructure.

output_examples.input: Plan tests for repository creation and cloning.
output: Unit tests cover valid and invalid repository names.
Integration tests create a test node, create a repository, and verify clone behavior.
CI runs the workspace test suite across supported Rust toolchains.
Property tests check round-trip behavior and invariant preservation.
A fuzz target feeds arbitrary bytes into message parsing.
The expected result is no panic and clear handling of invalid input.
Tests connect nodes, create a repository on one node, then wait for sync.
Assertions verify each node can observe the expected repository state.

best_practices: Review every command before running it in a local repository.
Keep fixtures isolated with temporary directories and deterministic setup.
Use property tests and fuzzing for parsers, signatures, and protocol boundaries.
anti_patterns: Do not run benchmark or fuzz commands in constrained CI jobs without limits.
Do not rely only on end-to-end tests for distributed system correctness.
Do not copy project-specific type names without adapting them to your codebase.

faq.question: What kind of project is this skill for?
faq.answer: It is aimed at Rust projects, especially distributed systems that use async code and repository workflows.

faq.question: Does the skill execute tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and patterns. The user or agent must choose when to run commands.

faq.question: Why is there a low security risk?
faq.answer: The skill includes local Cargo command examples. They are legitimate, but command execution should always be reviewed first.

faq.question: Can I use it outside the Guts project?
faq.answer: Yes, but examples use Guts-style names and should be adapted to the target project.

faq.question: Does it cover fuzz testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows a cargo-fuzz target pattern for parser inputs.

faq.question: Does it include CI guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a GitHub Actions matrix for operating systems and Rust toolchains.
